---
title: "Getting Quarto Started in NextJS"
date: 12/31/2024
date-format: "MMM D, YYYY"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
editor: visual
---

## Quarto

Quarto enables you to weave together content and executable code into a finished document. To learn more about Quarto see <https://quarto.org>.

## Running Code

When you click the **Render** button a document will be generated that includes both content and the output of embedded code. You can embed code like this:

```{r}
1 + 1
```

You can add options to executable code like this

```{r}
#| echo: false
2 * 2
```

The `echo: false` option disables the printing of code (only output is displayed).

```{r}
library(ggplot2)

x = seq(1:100)
y = seq(1:100)^2
df = data.frame(x,y)

ggplot(df,aes(x = x, y = y))+geom_point()

```

```{r}
library(ggplot2)
library(plotly)

x = seq(1:100)
y = seq(1:100)^2
df = data.frame(x,y)

p = ggplot(df,aes(x = x, y = y))+geom_point()

p = ggplotly(p)

p
```

this is my $s$ and this is my $\phi$

## workflow of adding a quarto blog to NextJs app

First, render the html using embed-resource: true in the YAML. Make sure to name the blog file a specific name that can be searched for like "2024-12-31-quarto-in-nextjs.qmd"

Then, copy that html file into the public folder.

Then, update the BlogSummary.tsx file. Make sure to add the description, the tags, the link to the public folder, and other information.

This is all the data needed to process the file and make it it's own web-page.

Then NextJS will do it's thing. The end product is a web-page with the similar TopNav. We will add a sidebar with links to all the other blogs, as well as my contact information at the bottom of the page. The aforementioned will be defined by the layout.tsx file.

The page.tsx file will then use iframe to supply the html files in the public folder to their own web-page.

So ingesting the iframe and making it typescript should give it it's own URL without the .html at the end.

The iframe will cover the full page.

Then we will have the TS
